prompt:
Four of our services talk to the Meridian platform API and each grew its own client-credentials handling. One fetches a token for every single request (the platform team noticed, loudly), one caches forever and starts throwing 401s an hour in, and two have thread races where a burst of jobs fires five token fetches at once. I want one vendored module, tokenflow.py, stdlib only, that all of them can adopt. test_tokenflow.py is the acceptance contract (it scripts a local mock of their token + resource endpoints); `python3 test_tokenflow.py` green means done, and the test file is read-only.

TokenManager(token_url, client_id, client_secret, *, clock=time.monotonic, skew=30.0)
- get_token() POSTs JSON {"grant_type": "client_credentials", "client_id": ..., "client_secret": ...} with Content-Type application/json (their gateway takes JSON, not form encoding) and reads {"access_token": ..., "expires_in": <seconds>} out of the 200.
- Cache it. The token counts as usable until fetch-time + expires_in - skew on the injectable clock; from that instant on (>= — the boundary itself refreshes, we lost a day to that once) the next get_token() fetches fresh. Nothing in this module may sleep or consult the wall clock on its own.
- Refresh guard: the manager must be thread-safe, and while one fetch is in flight every other caller waits for THAT fetch and shares its token. Two concurrent token requests from one manager is exactly the bug I'm killing.
- invalidate(token) is compare-and-clear: drop the cache only if the cached token equals the one passed in, so a worker holding yesterday's token can't wipe the fresh one somebody else just fetched.
- Token endpoint trouble — non-200, or a 200 with no access_token — raises TokenError carrying the endpoint's error detail in its message. A failed fetch must not leave the manager wedged; the next get_token() simply tries again.

ApiClient(base_url, manager)
- get_json(path): GET base_url+path with 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' using the manager's token, parse the JSON body, return it.
- On a 401, exactly once: invalidate the token it just used, get a fresh one, replay the request. A second 401 raises AuthError. Never a third attempt — that's how we got rate-limited in March.
- Any other non-2xx raises ApiError with a .status attribute. No retries and no backoff anywhere in this module; the service mesh owns transport-level retry policy.
- Make AuthError a subclass of ApiError (status 401) so callers can catch broadly; TokenError stays separate because it means config, not traffic.
